title: Mistral OCR 4
blurb: Mistral released OCR 4, a compact document-extraction model that returns bounding boxes, typed-block classification, and inline confidence scores alongside text across 170 languages. It runs in a single container for self-hosted deployments, tops OlmOCRBench at 85.20, and feeds structured, citation-ready output into RAG and enterprise search pipelines.

title: Boost Inference Performance up to 15x on NVIDIA Blackwell Using DFlash Speculative Decoding
blurb: NVIDIA released DFlash, an open-source block-diffusion drafter for speculative decoding that drafts an entire block of candidate tokens in one forward pass instead of sequentially. It boosts gpt-oss-120b inference on Blackwell by up to 15x at high interactivity, beats EAGLE-3 by 1.5x, and ships with 20 checkpoints on Hugging Face plus recipes for Blackwell and Hopper GPUs.

title: OpenAI Launches Daybreak To Automate Vulnerability Patching
blurb: OpenAI launched Daybreak, an AI system that automatically finds and patches security vulnerabilities in software. The tool scans codebases for exploitable flaws and generates fixes, extending OpenAI's push beyond chat assistants into autonomous security work and pitting it against a growing field of AI-driven code-auditing tools.

title: Claude Tag
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, a beta that lets teams add Claude to Slack channels as a taggable member with access to chosen tools, data, and codebases. Available now for Claude Enterprise and Team customers, it gives one shared Claude that builds context across a channel and works through delegated tasks, an approach Anthropic says already generates 65% of its product team's code internally.

title: China's LineShine supercomputer dethrones US' El Capitan, secures first place in Top 500 list — first machine in the rankings to sustain more than 2 ExaFLOPS of double-precision performance using only CPUs
blurb: China's LineShine supercomputer topped the Top 500 list at 2.198 FP64 ExaFLOPS on Linpack, dethroning the US El Capitan and becoming the first ranked system to sustain over 2 ExaFLOPS of double-precision performance using only CPUs. Built in Shenzhen from 13.79 million Armv9 cores across semi-custom 304-core LX2 chips, it leads on traditional HPC workloads but trails El Capitan, Frontier, and Aurora on mixed-precision benchmarks that matter for AI.

title: Interactions API: our primary interface for Gemini models and agents
blurb: Google announced that its Interactions API has reached general availability and is now the primary interface for building with Gemini models and agents, replacing the role-based schema with typed steps for inference, agents, and long-running background jobs. The GA release adds Managed Agents that provision a remote Linux sandbox, mixed built-in and custom tools, media generation with Nano Banana 2 and Lyria 3, and Flex and Priority tiers that cut costs up to 50%.

title: Moebius: 0.2B image inpainting model with 10B-level performance
blurb: Moebius is a 0.2B-parameter image inpainting model that matches 10B-scale quality using diffusion distillation from a high-capacity teacher.

title: Qwen-AgentWorld: Language World Models for General Agents
blurb: Qwen-AgentWorld is a family of open language world models that predict environment dynamics across seven agentic domains using long chain-of-thought reasoning, trained on over 10M real-world interaction trajectories. The 35B-A3B and 397B-A17B models can act as decoupled simulators for agentic RL, beating real-environment training alone, or as a warm-up that lifts downstream performance on seven agentic benchmarks.

title: LastPass confirms data breach in Klue supply chain attack
blurb: LastPass confirmed that attackers accessed customer data in its Salesforce environment after stealing OAuth tokens in the Klue supply chain breach, exposing names, phone numbers, email and physical addresses, and support case records. The company says its products, infrastructure, and customer vaults were unaffected, but the stolen CRM data raises phishing risk across the many firms Klue served, including Recorded Future, Tanium, and Gong.

title: Midjourney Medical
blurb: Midjourney, the AI image-generation startup, launched Midjourney Medical, a new effort applying its image models to healthcare and clinical imaging. The move marks the company's first expansion beyond consumer creative tools into a regulated vertical, where accuracy and safety requirements are far steeper than in general-purpose image generation.

title: Base Beryl: B20 Token Standard, Faster Withdrawals, Reth V2
blurb: Base Beryl testnet is live, adding the B20 token standard, faster withdrawals, and Reth V2 ahead of its June 25 mainnet upgrade.

title: How we found a bug in the hyper HTTP library
blurb: Cloudflare engineers spent six weeks tracing intermittent truncation in their Images service to a race condition in hyper, the Rust HTTP library, where large image responses returned a 200 status but arrived cut short by megabytes. The fix took four lines of code, and the writeup walks through how socket handoffs between the Workers runtime and the Images binding exposed the bug.

title: Anthropic’s Fable 5 Model Jailbroken Within Days
blurb: Anthropic's Fable 5, the guardrailed release of its Mythos Preview built to block requests for cyberattack code, had its safety restrictions bypassed within days of launch. The quick jailbreak underscores how brittle current alignment filters remain against determined red-teamers, putting pressure on Anthropic's claims that the model was safe to ship.

title: Run isolated sandboxes with full lifecycle control: AWS Lambda introduces MicroVMs
blurb: AWS announced Lambda MicroVMs, a serverless primitive that gives each user or session its own Firecracker-isolated, stateful execution environment with near-instant launch and resume. The product targets AI coding assistants, interactive code sandboxes, and other multi-tenant apps that run untrusted code, offering VM-level isolation without the minutes-long startup of traditional VMs or the custom hardening containers require.

title: Mandiant reveals how Cisco SD-WAN zero-day attacks gained root access
blurb: Mandiant detailed how attackers chained Cisco SD-WAN zero-day CVE-2026-20245 to escalate to root via a malicious tenant-upload CSV.

title: Malicious Edge extension abuses Native Messaging as bridge to malware
blurb: A malicious Edge extension called Edgecution abuses Chrome's Native Messaging protocol to escape the browser sandbox and deploy a Python backdoor in ransomware attacks.

title: Ethereum Foundation Cuts Budget 40% in Sweeping Restructuring
blurb: The Ethereum Foundation is cutting its annual budget by roughly 40% and laying off about 20% of staff, with 54 employees departing as part of a restructuring into five focused divisions. The shift moves the nonprofit toward an endowment model that spends roughly 5% of its funds annually after 2030, aiming to sustain Ethereum through downturns without relying on token sales.

title: Introducing computer use in Gemini 3.5 Flash
blurb: Google integrated computer use natively into Gemini 3.5 Flash, letting developers build agents that see, reason, and act across browser, mobile, and desktop environments. Previously a standalone Gemini 2.5 model, the capability now ships with two optional enterprise safeguards: required user confirmation for sensitive actions and automatic task termination when an indirect prompt injection is detected.

title: FortiBleed Attackers Turn Firewalls Into Credential Stealers as Heists Persist
blurb: Attackers behind the "FortiBleed" campaign deployed a Golang sniffer that exploits FortiOS's built-in diagnostic command to turn compromised FortiGate firewalls into passive credential collectors across 24 authentication protocols. SOCRadar links the campaign to roughly 430,000 exposed firewalls and 110 million harvested credentials since February, with victims including a NATO-aligned defense contractor, prompting a CISA advisory.

title: China’s LineShine dethrones El Capitan as the world’s fastest supercomputer
blurb: China's LineShine debuted atop the 67th TOP500 list at 2.198 exaflops, ending El Capitan's reign and giving China its first No. 1 system since 2017. Installed in Shenzhen, LineShine reached the top with a CPU-only domestic design built on China's LingKun platform, LingQi interconnect, and Kylin OS, sidestepping the AMD and Intel accelerators that power rival US systems.

title: Vulnerability reports are not special anymore
blurb: Filippo Valsorda, former lead of Go's Security team, argues that vulnerability reports no longer warrant the special treatment maintainers have long given them. His reasoning is that LLMs have made security insight cheap and confidentiality nearly worthless, shifting the bottleneck from finding potential issues to triaging which ones are real, where external researchers add little value without an existing trust relationship.

title: Unlimited OCR: One-Shot Long-Horizon Parsing
blurb: Baidu released Unlimited-OCR, a document-parsing model that extends Deepseek-OCR to handle long, multi-page inputs in a single pass. It runs on Hugging Face transformers with separate single-image and multi-page modes, supports a 32K-token output window, and parses full PDFs by converting pages to images before inference.

title: RFC 9958 - Post-Quantum Cryptography for Engineers
blurb: An IETF informational RFC explaining why engineers must understand post-quantum cryptography and how transitioning may force major protocol redesigns.

title: Nature publishes a peer-reviewed paper alleging that Microsoft's 2025 quantum breakthrough claims were based on "basic Python errors" and data cherry-picking (Thomas Claburn/The Register)
blurb: A peer-reviewed paper in Nature alleges that Microsoft's 2025 quantum computing breakthrough rested on basic Python errors and cherry-picked data, casting doubt on the company's headline claims. The critique, reported by The Register, argues the underlying analysis fails to support Microsoft's assertion that it had verified a topological qubit, reviving scrutiny of one of the field's most disputed milestones.

title: AWS debuts Lambda MicroVMs with up to 8 hours runtime
blurb: AWS launched Lambda MicroVMs, extending its serverless platform with isolated microVMs that can run for up to eight hours instead of the prior 15-minute function ceiling. The longer-running, hardware-isolated environments target untrusted code, AI agents, and other long jobs, positioning Lambda to compete with sandboxed-execution services as agent workloads push past traditional function limits.

title: Steam Machine launches today
blurb: Valve launched the Steam Machine, a compact living-room PC running SteamOS that brings the handheld Steam Deck's console-style experience to a TV. The release marks Valve's return to dedicated gaming hardware after the original Steam Machine line stalled a decade ago, positioning SteamOS as an alternative to Windows for couch gaming.

title: Sources: in a letter to US officials, Anthropic accused Alibaba of adversarial distillation, accessing Claude 28.8M times from April to June via ~25K accounts (Maggie Eastland/Bloomberg)
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba of adversarial distillation in a letter to US officials, alleging the Chinese company accessed Claude 28.8M times between April and June through roughly 25,000 accounts, according to Bloomberg. The claim escalates the fight over model distillation, where rivals query a frontier model to train competing systems, and feeds directly into Washington's broader scrutiny of Chinese AI labs.

title: Daybreak: Tools for securing every organization in the world
blurb: OpenAI expanded its Daybreak program with new tools to find and patch software vulnerabilities at machine speed, including a Codex Security plugin update and the full release of GPT-5.5-Cyber to trusted defenders. The model hits 85.6% on the CyberGym benchmark versus 81.8% for GPT-5.5, and a new Patch the Planet initiative with Trail of Bits has signed up 30-plus open-source projects including cURL, Go, and Python.

title: The text in Claude Code’s “Extended Thinking” output
blurb: The author found that Claude Code's locally saved "thinking blocks" contain only a 600-character encrypted signature, not readable text, because Anthropic encrypts the reasoning, holds the key, and returns only a summary unless you have an enterprise agreement. The practical warning: the extended-thinking output you can view is a lossy summary, not the actual reasoning that drove the agent, so it can't serve as a genuine audit trail.

title: Nearly half of LG smart TV apps contain residential proxy SDKs
blurb: Researchers at Spur scanned 6,038 LG and Samsung smart TV apps and found 2,058 of them, nearly half on LG, bundled residential proxy SDKs that route strangers' internet traffic through users' home networks. The apps, often calm-by-design tools like clocks and fish tanks, ask for consent once and keep selling the connection in the background after they're closed, with the proxy company frequently doubling as the publisher.

title: 29-Year-Old Squid Proxy Bug 'Squidbleed' Can Leak Cleartext HTTP Requests
blurb: A 29-year-old Squid proxy heap over-read, dubbed Squidbleed, leaks other users' cleartext HTTP requests and credentials.

title: White House drastically shortens deadline for dropping quantum-vulnerable crypto
blurb: A new White House executive order requires US government systems handling "high-value assets" and "high-impact systems" to adopt post-quantum key establishment by December 31, 2030 and quantum-safe signatures by 2031, roughly five years sooner than the prior 2035 deadline. The acceleration follows research showing a cryptographically relevant quantum computer would cost far less than once assumed, prompting Google and Cloudflare to move their own timelines to 2029.

title: OpenAI launches new security tools and updates GPT-5.5-Cyber
blurb: OpenAI launched a suite of defensive security tools centered on a shift from finding bugs to landing fixes, including an updated Codex Security plugin and the full GPT-5.5-Cyber model in limited release for verified defenders. Since its March preview, Codex Security has scanned over 30 million commits across 30,000 codebases, while GPT-5.5-Cyber scored 85.6% on CyberGym versus 81.8% for GPT-5.5.

title: Show HN: Oak – Git alternative designed for agents
blurb: Oak is a content-addressed, open-source version control system built for AI agents, using branch-per-session and lazy mounts to outpace git on agent workloads.

title: Nvidia's Autonomous Robotics Research
blurb: Nvidia's ENPIRE is a harness that lets coding agents run real-world robot learning as a closed loop, automatically resetting scenes, executing policies, verifying outcomes, and refining the next iteration. Using it, frontier coding agents autonomously developed manipulation policies reaching a 99% success rate on dexterous tasks like organizing pins and cutting zip ties, with optional acceleration across a parallel robot fleet.

title: Making secret scanning more trustworthy: Reducing false positives at scale
blurb: GitHub added LLM-based contextual verification to its secret scanning, cutting false positives by reasoning about how a detected value appears in code.

title: Ethereum Foundation’s Interim ED Lays Out Execution Plan: MEV Elimination, Default Privacy, ETH Pay
blurb: Ethereum Foundation's interim ED laid out a six-part plan to eliminate toxic MEV, make privacy a protocol default, and pay staff in ETH.

title: FortiBleed Targeted FortiGate Firewalls in 110 Million-Credential Harvesting Operation
blurb: A Russian-speaking access broker harvested over 110 million credentials via FortiBleed, deploying passive sniffers on 430,000-plus FortiGate firewalls since February.

title: CrossPool: Efficient Multi-LLM Serving for Cold MoE Models through KV-Cache and Weight Disaggregation
blurb: CrossPool is a serving engine for cold MoE models that splits FFN weights and KV-cache into two separate GPU memory pools, so static weights stop competing with transient cache. The design adds a KV-cache planner, a layer-wise pipeline scheduler that hides hidden-state transfers, and persistent kernels, cutting P99 time-between-tokens by up to 10.4x over the leading kvcached-based multi-LLM system.

title: Bayesian control for coding agents
blurb: This paper formulates coding-agent orchestration as cost-sensitive sequential hypothesis testing, replacing fixed tool-use rules with a Bayesian controller that maintains a belief over candidate correctness and decides whether to gather more evidence, refine, verify, or stop. Across six generators and nine benchmarks, the approach helped most when verification was costly and critics were informative but imperfect, and its belief state also produced a correctness score that beat token-probability and tool-success baselines for uncertainty estimates.

title: Microsoft discovers new lightweight backdoor that steals cryptocurrency
blurb: Microsoft detected Crypto Clipper, a self-propagating worm that spreads via USB drives and steals cryptocurrency by scanning device clipboards for wallet addresses and seed phrases. The malware exfiltrates stolen credentials and screenshots over Tor through a local SOCKS5 proxy, and pairs the theft with remote code execution, turning a financially motivated stealer into a lightweight backdoor with no traditional installer or IP-based command infrastructure.

title: Malicious hackers exploit Cisco zero-day for highest access level at communications service provider
blurb: Attackers exploited an unpatched Cisco SD-WAN zero-day to gain root access at a communications provider, Mandiant reported.

title: Amadey and StealC Malware Network Disrupted, 27M Stolen Credentials Recovered
blurb: Law enforcement and security firms dismantled the Amadey and StealC malware-as-a-service network, seizing 326 servers and recovering 27M stolen credentials.

title: Adopting AV1 for Real-Time Communication (RTC) at Scale
blurb: Meta has enabled the AV1 video codec on the majority of mobile devices in its RTC apps, including Messenger and WhatsApp, after introducing it for high-end devices in 2023. The codec delivers at least a 20% bitrate reduction versus H.264/AVC, keeping video calls clearer on slow or limited networks where bitrates often fall between 10 and 400 kbps.

title: Thinking to recall: How reasoning unlocks parametric knowledge in LLMs
blurb: Google Research published "Thinking to Recall," showing that chain-of-thought reasoning helps LLMs retrieve simple stored facts even when no multi-step deduction is required. The authors trace the effect to two mechanisms: reasoning tokens act as a computational buffer for latent processing, and generated related facts prime correct recall, with pass@k measurements confirming that reasoning surfaces answers otherwise unreachable in top-1 output.

title: Figma adds code layers, support for animations, more AI features in new update
blurb: Figma released an update that adds code layers to its collaborative canvas, native support for animations, transitions, and 3D transforms, and AI tools for generating shaders, custom plug-ins, and reusable agent skills. The code layers let teams clone repositories and pull flows into design layers for rapid iteration, while connections to Notion, Excel, and GitHub give Figma's AI assistant more context to act on.

title: FUTO Swipe – A new swipe typing model
blurb: FUTO released a new swipe typing model along with the open dataset behind it, built from over 1 million QWERTY English swipes collected from volunteers who traced Wikipedia sentences word-by-word. The 1-million-swipe dataset shipped in March 2025 under the MIT license on HuggingFace, giving researchers a rare open corpus for training and benchmarking gesture typing systems.

title: GLM-5.2 Raises the Bar for Open Models
blurb: GLM-5.2 launched last week as likely the strongest open model available, a clear step up from GLM-5.1 and closer to the absolute frontier than DeepSeek R1 was at its peak. By release date alone it trails the frontier by roughly four to seven months at a lower price, though benchmarks mark a ceiling rather than real-world performance, and gaps like missing vision limit its practical niche.

title: A new paper argues Microsoft exaggerated its quantum claims a year ago
blurb: A peer-reviewed critique in Nature argues that Microsoft's 2025 Majorana 1 chip did not conclusively demonstrate a working topological qubit, the technology Microsoft has positioned as the building block for its future quantum computer. Physicist Henry Legg reanalyzed Microsoft's device data and disputes the central claim; Microsoft rejects the critique and showed a next-generation Majorana 2 chip at Build this month.

title: NYSE Parent ICE and OKX Form 50-50 Joint Venture for Tokenized Equities, Co-Chaired by Andrew Cuomo
blurb: Intercontinental Exchange, the parent of the New York Stock Exchange, and crypto exchange OKX are forming a 50-50 joint venture to bring NYSE-listed equities on-chain, co-chaired by former New York Governor Andrew Cuomo. Pending regulatory approval, the venture will operate as a US registered broker-dealer giving OKX's 120M-plus customers access to ICE futures and tokenized NYSE equities, extending ICE's $25B bet on OKX from March.

title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
blurb: Two Scattered Spider members, Thalha Jubair, 20, and Owen Flowers, 18, pleaded guilty in the UK to the August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, folding on the first day of an expected six-week trial. Jubair is also wanted in the US, where a New Jersey indictment ties Scattered Spider to 120 network intrusions across 47 entities and at least $115M in ransom payments.

title: There are a few things that I look back on as my mistakes in the early days
blurb: John Carmack reflected on his mistakes building Quake at id Software, calling the game overly ambitious technically, admitting he pushed the team too hard at unsustainable startup intensity, and faulting the founders' stock buy/sell arrangement for creating bad incentives. He argued standard Silicon Valley vesting would have worked better, and acknowledged id should have paired artists with level designers far earlier to defuse the infighting that fractured the design team.

title: I rewrote PostHog's SQL parser, 70x faster, while barely looking at the code
blurb: A PostHog engineer rewrote the company's ANTLR-generated SQL parser as a hand-rolled C++ parser using parallel long-running Claude Code sessions, producing 16K lines of parser code and a roughly 70x speedup. The new parser matches the old one for all realistic queries and only diverges on pathological inputs, trading ANTLR's per-token interpretation overhead for purpose-built code the author barely reviewed.

title: China tops the list of fastest supercomputers with a CPU-only behemoth, ending US champion El Capitan's reign — 2.198 exaflops of performance without a single GPU
blurb: China's LineShine supercomputer topped the 67th TOP500 list at 2.198 exaflops on the Linpack benchmark, beating AMD-powered El Capitan by more than 20% using no GPUs or accelerators. The system reached two exaflops with 13.8M cores of domestic Armv9 silicon, making it the first CPU-only machine past that mark and the first China-based leader since 2017.

title: Anthropic Accuses Alibaba of ‘Illicitly’ Accessing AI Models
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba of running a large-scale effort to illicitly access its Claude models through thousands of fraudulent accounts. The campaign aimed to circumvent Anthropic's decision to keep its products out of China, highlighting the tension between US labs guarding frontier models and Chinese firms seeking access to them.

title: Microsoft uses AI to link two malware operations in racketeering suit
blurb: Microsoft used AI to connect two malware operations in a racketeering lawsuit, arguing the same actors ran both campaigns. The case marks an expanding use of machine learning in legal attribution, where automated analysis of code and infrastructure patterns becomes evidence rather than just a threat-hunting tool.

title: Trump sets post-quantum crypto deadlines, launches broader federal quantum initiative
blurb: Trump signed two executive orders setting federal deadlines for migrating to post-quantum cryptography and directing agencies to inventory their cryptographic assets. The orders signal coming procurement requirements that will push contractors and critical-infrastructure operators to prove readiness, citing a "harvest now, decrypt later" threat in which adversaries store encrypted data today to decrypt once powerful quantum computers arrive.

title: Evaluating the Interpretability of Sparse Autoencoders with Concept Annotations
blurb: A human-grounded framework for evaluating sparse autoencoder interpretability finds that moderate dictionary sizes yield the most interpretable concepts.

title: Cyclic Denoising Reveals Ultrastable Memories in Diffusion Models
blurb: Researchers introduced cyclic denoising, an extraction attack that repeatedly noises and denoises a diffusion model's outputs to surface "ultrastable" attractors that regenerate after near-total corruption and persist through thousands of cycles. Many of these attractors turn out to be memorized training images, including stock photos, brand watermarks, and web-crawl artifacts, and the attack needs only sampler control, with no gradients, prompts, or prior knowledge of the training set.

title: How GPT-5 helped immunologist Derya Unutmaz solve a 3-year-old mystery
blurb: OpenAI shared an account of how immunologist Derya Unutmaz used GPT-5 Pro to crack a T cell behavior question that had stumped his lab for three years. The model proposed a mechanistic hypothesis that fit the experimental data, work the team says could feed into cancer and autoimmune disease research.

title: Scattered Spider members plead guilty to hacking Transport for London
blurb: Two Scattered Spider members, Thalha Jubair (20) and Owen Flowers (18), pleaded guilty to the 2024 hack of Transport for London, which forced all 28,000 employees to reset passwords in person and caused £29M ($38.3M) in damage. UK investigators credited TfL's early engagement with law enforcement for the conviction and have linked Flowers to intrusions at US healthcare providers SSM Health and Sutter Health.

title: Chinese supercomputer using local processors heads TOP500 list
blurb: A Chinese supercomputer built entirely on domestically designed processors took the top spot on the latest TOP500 ranking, marking the first time a non-US, non-Western chip architecture leads the global list. The result underscores how export controls have accelerated China's push toward homegrown HPC silicon, reducing its reliance on Nvidia, AMD, and Intel hardware for frontier computing.

title: Accelerating BEV Pooling on NVIDIA GPUs for Physical AI Applications
blurb: NVIDIA published a technical walkthrough of BEVPoolV3, an optimized kernel for bird's-eye-view pooling, the step that fuses multicamera image features into a unified top-down grid for autonomous vehicles and robots. The post details how reducing duplicate depth loads, using a five-array INT32 scatter map, and profiling with Nsight Compute cut latency, since the operator needs different strategies depending on whether its working set is DRAM-bound or L2-resident.

title: Large-Language-Model Discovery of Quantum LDPC Codes through Structured Concept Evolution
blurb: Researchers introduced structured concept evolution (SCE), a search framework that pairs a large language model with an algebraic mutation grammar to discover quantum low-density parity-check codes, a leading approach to correcting the pervasive errors in quantum hardware. SCE evolved competitive lifted-product code families spanning abelian and non-abelian groups beyond standard bivariate-bicycle designs, achieving the results with lightweight models like GPT-5.4-mini and GPT-5.4-nano.

title: Agentic Generation of AST Transformation Rules for Fixing Breaking Updates
blurb: BigBag is an agentic framework that generates reusable AST transformation rules to repair code broken by third-party library updates, encoding fix logic at the API level so a single rule transfers across every project broken by the same change. Tested on 157 compilation failures from the BUMP benchmark, its best configuration hit a 78.6% fix rate and repaired 80% or more of clients when all invoked the affected API uniformly.

title: Swift Package Index joins Apple
blurb: Apple acquired the Swift Package Index, the community-run directory that catalogs and tests thousands of open-source Swift and SwiftUI packages. The site will keep running as an independent resource with its existing team, while Apple's backing secures funding and infrastructure for a tool that had been maintained largely through sponsorships and volunteer work.

title: Mythos discovers 'Squidbleed,' a memory leak that's gone undetected since Clinton era
blurb: Mythos disclosed "Squidbleed," a memory leak in Squid proxy code that went undetected since the 1990s.

title: Qualcomm to Buy Modular for $3.9B to Help AI Push
blurb: Qualcomm agreed to acquire AI software company Modular for about $3.9B in stock, adding model-deployment and inference tooling to support its push into data centers. The deal gives Qualcomm software to pair with its chip ambitions as it tries to compete with Nvidia and AMD for AI infrastructure spend beyond its core mobile and edge markets.

title: Qualcomm to Acquire AI Software Firm Modular in $3.9 Billion Stock Deal
blurb: Qualcomm agreed to acquire AI software firm Modular for about $3.9 billion in stock, aiming to make AI inference faster and cheaper across its hardware. The deal pairs Modular's developer tooling, built to run AI workloads across different chips, with Qualcomm's push into data center and edge AI beyond its core smartphone business.

title: U.S. Presses Meta to Agree to A.I. Reviews
blurb: Federal officials are pressing Meta to allow government safety evaluations of its AI models, leaving it the last major tech company yet to agree. The request follows an order weeks earlier forcing Anthropic to pull its latest model, signaling that Washington is moving toward mandatory pre-release reviews across the frontier labs.

title: Decentralised AI Training and Inference with BlockTrain
blurb: Spheroid BlockTrain is a decentralized training protocol that splits a model into independently trainable blocks, each optimized on a local objective and reassembled at inference, letting workers train one block without holding full-model optimizer state. On byte-level WikiText it reaches cross entropy 1.359, within 0.04 of an end-to-end Transformer, and serves inference across three public-IP GPU hosts up to a 75.8B-parameter fp16 shape.

title: Poisoned Playbooks: Demystifying Knowledge Poisoning Effects on AI Security Agents
blurb: A single poisoned write-up in public security knowledge can systematically alter RAG-based AI security agents' exploit behavior across CTF challenges and real CVEs.

title: F3
blurb: F3 is a research-prototype columnar file format that fixes Parquet's layout limits while staying future-proof through embedded Wasm decoders.

title: Germany went off the rails as wireless outage saw all trains cancelled
blurb: A failure in Germany's GSM-R rail radio network forced Deutsche Bahn to cancel trains nationwide, leaving passengers stranded when the wireless system that dispatchers use to communicate with drivers went down. The outage underscores how dependent modern rail operations are on a single legacy radio standard, with no fallback capable of keeping trains moving once the link drops.

title: Spotlight on WG Device Management
blurb: Kubernetes' Dynamic Resource Allocation graduated to GA, letting workloads request GPUs, TPUs, and other hardware beyond CPU and memory.

title: InSight: Self-Guided Skill Acquisition via Steerable VLAs
blurb: InSight is a framework that lets robots acquire manipulation skills without human demonstrations of the target tasks. It makes vision-language-action models steerable at the level of primitive actions like "lift upward" or "pour the bottle," then uses a VLM-guided data flywheel to identify missing primitives, attempt them autonomously, and fold successful demonstrations back into training. The learned primitives compose into novel long-horizon tasks including block flipping, drawer closing, and sweeping.

title: Fired by Google for creating the Google workspace CLI
blurb: Justin Poehnelt says Google fired him two months ago over a personal Google Workspace CLI that hit #1 on Hacker News and drew thousands of GitHub stars and users within days. He attributes the termination to Workspace leaders' fear of agent-driven disruption, noting Google announced its own official Workspace CLI at Cloud Next two days before he was let go.

title: Lift4D: Harmonizing Single-View 3D Estimation for 4D Reconstruction In-the-Wild
blurb: Lift4D is a test-time optimization framework that reconstructs complete, dynamic 3D objects from a single monocular video, handling severe occlusions and non-rigid motion that defeat prior methods. It adapts a single-view 3D model for temporally consistent per-frame predictions, then sculpts a deformable 3D Gaussian Splatting representation that recovers visible surfaces and fills occluded regions using a view-conditioned diffusion prior.

title: Elevated error rate across multiple models
blurb: Anthropic reported an elevated error rate across multiple Claude models on its status page, with the incident drawing attention on Hacker News. The disruption underscores how dependent production tooling and agent workflows have become on a handful of frontier model providers, where a single upstream incident can ripple into downstream apps that route requests through one API.

title: Production-Ready Autonomous Incident Resolution with AWS DevOps Agent (now GA) and Datadog MCP Server
blurb: AWS DevOps Agent is now generally available, pairing with the GA Datadog MCP Server for autonomous incident triage across AWS, multicloud, and on-prem.

title: Why American data centers can't plug in
blurb: American data centers face an electricity bottleneck that stems from grid connection, not generation, as interconnection wait times for new power plants jumped from under 20 months in 2005 to 55 months by 2023. The author argues that first-come, first-served queues and rigid evaluation rules now block the most valuable AI projects, and that grid interconnection processes must be redesigned to handle loads like OpenAI's 1.2-gigawatt Stargate campus.

title: Zelle to Launch Stablecoin and Expand to India
blurb: Zelle will launch a US dollar-backed stablecoin called ZelleUSD (ZLUSD) later this year and is expanding its app to India, though without the stablecoin in that market. The move puts the seven banks behind Zelle's parent Early Warning, including JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America, and Wells Fargo, directly into stablecoins, with ZLUSD aimed at cheaper international remittances to corridors like India, Mexico, and Argentina.

title: Alibaba's model never trained as an agent — and improved agent performance across seven benchmarks
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen team released Qwen-AgentWorld, two models trained to predict what agent environments return rather than to act inside them, spanning seven domains including MCP, Search, Terminal, and Android under one architecture. Trained on more than 10 million real interaction trajectories, the models improved agent performance across seven benchmarks, including three never seen in training, when used as a warm-up before agentic fine-tuning.

title: Qualcomm’s $3.9 billion purchase of Modular aims to change the data center dynamic
blurb: Qualcomm will acquire AI-software company Modular for $3.9 billion in stock to build a "silicon-agnostic compute layer" spanning edge, cloud, CPU, GPU, NPU, and custom ASICs. Modular, founded by Chris Lattner to scale software across heterogeneous AI hardware, is meant to give Qualcomm a path into data centers, though analysts doubt it will pull meaningful share from Nvidia.

title: [AINews] Claude Tag: Multiplayer, Proactive, Persistent Agents in Slack
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, a Slack-native way to delegate work to Claude as an asynchronous teammate, marking what the company calls the third major redesign of its chat interface after web and desktop. Tag joins selected channels with access to chosen tools and codebases, can loop in coworkers who own related code, wait on git webhooks for days, and run ambient routines that respond and fix issues without being directly tagged.

title: Scope of Salesforce Attacks Expands as Icarus Leaks Data
blurb: Attackers breached market-intelligence vendor Klue and abused its OAuth tokens to steal Salesforce data from its customers, with extortion group Icarus claiming credit and warning of more victims. LastPass, Huntress, HackerOne, Snyk, Jamf, Tanium, and others have disclosed compromised Salesforce instances, underscoring how a single third-party integration can cascade breaches across dozens of downstream companies.

title: Zuckerberg Orders Meta to Build Standalone Prediction Market App, Codenamed Arena
blurb: Mark Zuckerberg has directed a small internal team to build a standalone Meta prediction-market app codenamed Arena, distinct from Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger. The app launches with a video game-style points system rather than real-money betting, but Meta's 3.56 billion daily active users dwarf crypto-native rivals Polymarket and Kalshi, and the news pushed Robinhood and DraftKings shares lower.

title: Congress Passes Fed CBDC Ban Through 2030, Sends Bill to Trump
blurb: Congress passed a ban on the Fed issuing a central bank digital currency through 2030, sending the bill to Trump.

title: 21,000 Oracle jobs vanish amid Big Red's big bets on AI
blurb: Oracle's headcount fell from 162,000 to 141,000 over the past year, a roughly 21,000-job drop disclosed in its annual report. The cuts come as Oracle pours billions into datacenter expansion to chase AI demand, trading payroll for compute capacity to compete with hyperscalers on cloud infrastructure.

title: One-two punch delivered in global operation disrupts cybercrime "assembly line"
blurb: International authorities and several private tech companies disrupted a cybercrime "assembly line" built on two tools, Amadey and StealC, that together stole login credentials and over $47M in ransom and fraud. Microsoft used AI to find that the otherwise separate malware-as-a-service platforms shared underlying infrastructure, letting its attorneys secure a single order to take down both at once.

title: Meta ships first smart glasses powered by its Superintelligence Labs’ Muse Spark model
blurb: Meta partnered with EssilorLuxottica to launch a new line of AI smart glasses starting at $299, about $80 below the last Ray-Ban Meta lineup and powered by Muse Spark, the first multimodal reasoning model from Meta Superintelligence Labs. The glasses add a dedicated Meta AI button, eight-hour battery, live translation in 14 new languages, and planned turn-by-turn pedestrian navigation, though Meta's earlier facial-recognition testing still shadows its privacy claims.

title: Broadcom stock needs a win. The new OpenAI co-designed Jalapeno chip might do the trick
blurb: Broadcom and OpenAI introduced Jalapeno, their first co-designed AI accelerator chip, aimed at lowering token costs by improving performance per watt for a joint AI platform. The announcement lands as Broadcom stock sits down nearly 20% since early June, with the deal offering clearer deployment timelines after reports that Alphabet and ByteDance are exploring other silicon design partners.

title: AdversaBench: Automated LLM Red-Teaming with Multi-Judge Confirmation and Cross-Model Transferability
blurb: AdversaBench automates LLM red-teaming with five mutation operators and a three-judge panel; every test seed produced a confirmed failure, transferring across model sizes.

title: Google is finally opening the Play Store to outside payments
blurb: Google will replace Play Store's flat 30% billing fee with lower, decoupled rates and let Android developers route users to outside payment systems, part of the unsigned settlement of Epic's antitrust suit. Apps earning over $1M annually will pay 20% on new in-app purchases and 10% on subscriptions, with further reductions for those that meet Google's cross-platform and performance benchmarks.

title: Unsloth GLM-5.2 – How to Run Locally
blurb: Z.ai's open GLM-5.2 (744B params, 1M context) runs locally via Unsloth dynamic quants, fitting on a 256GB Mac.

title: Flock-Powered Police Chiefs Stalking Women Shows Why Warrants Are Needed
blurb: An Illinois police chief was arrested and charged with two felony counts of official misconduct after prosecutors said he used Prairie Grove's Flock license plate readers and a state database to track six people he knew, including three former romantic partners. The Institute for Justice has counted at least 18 similar cases of officers using Flock to stalk partners and rivals, arguing the pattern is why such searches should require a warrant.

title: Sakana Fugu
blurb: Sakana released Fugu, an LLM trained to orchestrate a pool of swappable models, including recursive instances of itself, behind a single OpenAI-compatible endpoint. Fugu handles selection, delegation, verification, and synthesis automatically and ships in two tiers: a low-latency version for everyday coding and chat, and Fugu Ultra for hard multi-step work like AI research, cybersecurity, and patent analysis.

title: Crypto Heist Fueled by Elaborate Fake Reputation-Boosting Campaign
blurb: Check Point uncovered a crypto clipboard hijacker spread through fake GitHub, SourceForge, and YouTube assets built to fake legitimacy.

title: Linux Foundation prepares open standard for AI agent verification
blurb: The Linux Foundation is developing an open standard called the Agent Name Service to let enterprises verify and identify AI agents operating across the internet. Modeled on DNS, the protocol aims to give companies a vendor-neutral way to authenticate agents at scale as autonomous systems begin transacting with each other and with external services.

title: Cloudflare teams up with big browsers to help websites tell welcome from unwelcome visitors
blurb: Cloudflare is working with major browser makers on a standard letting websites distinguish legitimate visitors and bots from unwelcome traffic.

title: Cloudflare partners with Google, Microsoft, and Mozilla on PACT, a protocol to distinguish legitimate human or bot traffic from undesirable network requests (Thomas Claburn/The Register)
blurb: Cloudflare partnered with Google, Microsoft, and Mozilla on PACT, a protocol that lets websites distinguish legitimate human and bot traffic from undesirable network requests. The standard aims to give site operators a shared way to verify well-behaved automated agents and crawlers, a growing concern as AI bots flood the web and strain infrastructure.

title: Trump executive orders speed up post-quantum migration, boost industry
blurb: Trump signed two executive orders Monday accelerating the federal government's shift to post-quantum encryption and redirecting financing toward the domestic quantum computing industry. One order pushes federal civilian networks to adopt NIST-vetted quantum-resistant algorithms ahead of the current 2035 deadline, while the broader effort follows more than $2B in CHIPS Act financing incentives already pledged to nine quantum companies.

title: AWS unveils sovereign-centric micro serverless virtual machines
blurb: AWS released Lambda MicroVMs, a new resource that gives each end user or session its own isolated Firecracker-backed environment for running untrusted or AI-generated code. Each machine offers up to 16 vCPUs, 32GB of memory, and eight hours of runtime with suspendable state, targeting developers who need sovereignty and security controls without managing virtualization infrastructure.

title: A new supercomputer has dethroned the U.S — here’s why it matters
blurb: China's LineShine supercomputer in Shenzhen topped the latest Top500 list at over 2.1 exaflops, displacing the US Department of Energy's El Capitan and becoming the first Chinese system to rank first since 2017. Unusually, LineShine hit that mark using only traditional CPUs rather than the GPUs that power most AI workloads, a reminder that raw computing leadership and AI accelerator dominance are not the same race.

title: Raspberry Pi Pico W as USB Wi-Fi Adapter
blurb: This project turns a $6 Raspberry Pi Pico W into a USB Wi-Fi adapter, exposing its onboard CYW43 wireless chip to a host computer over USB. The firmware presents the Pico as a standard network device, offering a cheap way to add Wi-Fi to machines that lack it or to experiment with low-level USB and wireless networking on commodity hardware.

title: Investors get real-time view of UK bond market activity for the first time
blurb: The UK launched a consolidated tape for bonds, giving investors a single real-time view of trades for the first time outside North America.

title: ClickHouse Ingestion at Scale: An Open-Source Zepto Engineering Story
blurb: Zepto rebuilt its Kafka-to-ClickHouse ingestion pipeline after Confluent Cloud's managed setup blocked the low-level broker and connector tuning it needed to sustain 10 MB/s of event traffic. By adding batching and buffering logic to the open-source ClickHouse Kafka Connect connector, the team boosted ingestion 45%, eliminated GC pauses, and cut insert pressure, contributing the fixes back upstream in two merged pull requests.

title: Why Zelle wants to launch a stablecoin
blurb: Zelle launched Zelle USD, a stablecoin for international payments, a week after rival bank consortium TCH committed to tokenized deposits instead. Owned by seven banks including JPMorgan and Wells Fargo, Zelle processed over $1.2T in domestic volume in 2025, and its move signals that big banks now see public-blockchain stablecoins as the cross-border rail that tokenized deposits can't serve.

title: Cloudflare teams up with Chrome, Firefox, and Edge on a privacy-first anti-bot protocol
blurb: Cloudflare, Mozilla, Google, Microsoft, and Shopify are building PACT, a privacy-first protocol that issues anonymous tokens to verify a visitor is human or an authorized bot without CAPTCHAs, logins, or tracking. The group plans to submit it for formal standardization as bot traffic overtakes humans online, with Cloudflare data showing automated systems now drive roughly 58% of HTTP requests worldwide.

title: LLM-Driven Feature Discovery
blurb: A method for cataloging an LLM's behaviors by having an autorater extract per-transcript features, then embedding and clustering them, no model internals required.

title: Trump Order Sets 2030 Deadline for Federal Post-Quantum Crypto Migration
blurb: Trump signed an executive order on June 22 setting hard deadlines for federal agencies to migrate high-value systems to post-quantum cryptography, with key establishment due by the end of 2030 and digital signatures by the end of 2031. The order pulls the government's timeline forward four to five years to counter "harvest now, decrypt later" attacks, and turns NIST's 2024 ML-KEM and ML-DSA standards into a schedule with consequences for agencies and contractors alike.

title: 45°C cooling design cuts data center water use to near zero
blurb: NVIDIA's Rubin generation is the first AI infrastructure to use 100% liquid cooling, running coolant up to 45°C with no fans anywhere in the closed-loop system. In favorable climates the design enables chiller-less dry-cooler operation, cutting facility water use from roughly 2.6 million gallons per megawatt per year to near zero and trimming cooling energy that has historically reached 40% of data center electricity.

title: Deno project is going to add cross-platform desktop apps in next major update
blurb: The Deno project will add cross-platform desktop app support in its next major release, letting developers build native-feeling apps with web technologies on the JavaScript and TypeScript runtime. The feature aims to compete with Electron and Tauri by bundling Deno's existing tooling, positioning the runtime as a single stack for server, CLI, and desktop work.

title: RubyLLM: A Ruby framework for all major AI providers
blurb: RubyLLM is a Ruby framework that wraps GPT, Claude, and local Ollama behind one interface, with just three dependencies.

title: Google Poised to Lose Two More Senior AI Staffers to Anthropic
blurb: Two senior Google AI researchers, Jonas Adler and Alexander Pritzel, both viewed internally as key contributors to the Gemini model, are planning to leave for Anthropic. The moves add to a string of high-profile departures that threaten Google's competitive standing in AI, as Anthropic continues to pull frontier talent from rival labs.

title: Linux Foundation extends DNS to AI agents with new Agent Name Service
blurb: The Linux Foundation announced the Agent Name Service, an open standard that gives AI agents verifiable identities through DNS rather than a new registry or lookup network. ANS lets organizations confirm which entity an agent belongs to, what it is permitted to do, and whether its code or activity log has been tampered with, with GoDaddy, Cloudflare, Cisco, and Salesforce backing the effort.

title: Dell launches AI server based on Nvidia Vera Rubin GPUs
blurb: Dell launched the PowerEdge XE8812, a liquid-cooled AI server built on Nvidia's Vera Rubin NVL4 architecture that scales to 144 GPUs per rack. It anchors the Dell AI Factory with Nvidia bundle and ships with 50% more memory per socket and GPU than the prior GB200 generation, letting enterprises run larger models and HPC simulations entirely in-memory.

title: The Coming Loop
blurb: The author describes a shift in agentic engineering from prompting coding agents directly to writing harness-level loops that queue work, attempt it, judge whether an agent's "I'm done" was real, and keep the task alive across sessions or machines. He argues this approach still fails for code he cares about, since present-day models produce overly defensive, locally reasoned code that adds fallbacks instead of making bad states impossible.

title: Digital euro clears key hurdle as EU seeks to break free from U.S. credit cards
blurb: The European Parliament's economic committee approved draft rules for a digital euro, advancing the ECB's plan for a central-bank-backed electronic wallet that would cut the euro zone's reliance on Visa and Mastercard. The ECB plans a 12-month pilot starting in late 2026 ahead of a full 2029 launch, with final legislative approval targeted by year-end amid concerns over bank deposit outflows and U.S. payment-network dominance.

title: The minimum viable unit of saleable software
blurb: The author argues that LLMs have collapsed the cost of building software, upending the old buy-vs-build calculus that once favored buying anything outside a company's core domain. The thesis is that cheap is not zero: LLM-built systems still demand iterative feedback loops and ongoing maintenance, leaving room for small, sustainable software businesses even when an internal Claude-built clone is a few prompts away.

title: Announcing DuckDB 1.5.4
blurb: DuckDB 1.5.4 (Variegata) ships correctness fixes, performance improvements, and security patches, alongside a parallel v1.4.5 LTS release.

title: Apple patches Beats Studio Buds flaw that could turn earbuds into a wiretap
blurb: Apple patched CVE-2025-20701, a Bluetooth authentication flaw in Beats Studio Buds that let an attacker within range connect to earbuds in pairing mode and listen through their microphone before pairing completed. The bug stems from Airoha SoCs used across many audio products, and Apple's automatic Beats Firmware Update 1B211 closes it, though real-world exploitation requires proximity and specialized skill.

title: The memory crisis is getting so bad that even retro RAM prices are going to the Moon
blurb: The ongoing memory shortage has spread beyond modern DRAM, with prices for decades-old RAM chips used in retro and vintage computers now climbing sharply. The Register attributes the squeeze to AI datacenter demand absorbing fab capacity, which ripples down to the small-batch and legacy modules that hobbyists and repair shops rely on, leaving no corner of the memory market untouched.

title: OpenAI gets chippy with Broadcom
blurb: OpenAI is partnering with Broadcom to design its own custom AI accelerators, deepening a move to build silicon in-house rather than rely solely on Nvidia. The deal gives OpenAI more control over cost and supply for training and inference, while handing Broadcom a marquee customer for its custom-chip business as hyperscalers race to cut their dependence on Nvidia GPUs.

title: Micron reports Q3 revenue up 346% YoY to $41.46B, vs. $35.84B est., gross margin above estimates, and forecasts Q4 revenue above est.; MU jumps 14%+ after hours (Kif Leswing/CNBC)
blurb: Micron reported Q3 revenue of $41.46B, up 346% year over year and ahead of the $35.84B estimate, with gross margin also topping expectations on surging memory demand. The company guided Q4 revenue above consensus, and shares jumped more than 14% after hours as AI-driven appetite for high-bandwidth memory continues to tighten supply.

title: Accenture snaps up majority stake in Dragos, acquires runZero and NetRise in critical infrastructure security push
blurb: Accenture agreed to acquire a majority stake in OT security firm Dragos plus all of runZero and NetRise, a triple deal worth roughly $4.175B. Dragos will stay independent under CEO Robert M. Lee while absorbing runZero's exposure assessment and NetRise's supply-chain security, pushing Accenture into OT cybersecurity software in a market it estimates will hit $59B by 2031.

title: Report: Kennedy Space Center not ready for era of super heavy rockets
blurb: A NASA Inspector General report found that launch infrastructure at Kennedy Space Center is aging and lacks the capacity to meet rising demand from SpaceX's Starship and Blue Origin's New Glenn. Kennedy has only a handful of pads, including Launch Complex 39A leased to SpaceX, leaving roads, gas lines, and other facilities strained as super heavy rockets enter regular service.

title: Sakana AI releases Fugu Ultra system to rival top AI labs
blurb: Sakana AI released Fugu Ultra, a frontier orchestration model that delegates subtasks to a pool of expert LLMs, available worldwide through a single OpenAI-compatible API. The company pitches it as an AI-sovereignty play for demanding multi-step work in engineering, science, and cybersecurity, claiming benchmark parity with Anthropic's Fable 5 while avoiding single-vendor and export-control risk.

title: Solving Inverse Problems of Chaotic Systems with Bidirectional Conditional Flow Matching
blurb: Bi-CFM solves inverse problems in chaotic systems, inferring initial conditions from final states with a 100x+ speedup over baselines.

title: Qualcomm unveils Dragonfly C1000, a new data center CPU built for agentic AI, and says Meta will use the chip when production starts in 2028 (Kif Leswing/CNBC)
blurb: Qualcomm introduced the Dragonfly C1000, a data center CPU designed for agentic AI workloads, and said Meta has committed to using the chip once production begins in 2028. The move pushes Qualcomm deeper into the server market long dominated by Intel, AMD, and Nvidia, with a marquee customer secured years ahead of shipment.

title: Rhombus Language 1.0
blurb: Rhombus 1.0 is now available, a general-purpose functional language from the Racket team that pairs conventional, non-parenthesized syntax with Racket's macro-based extensibility. The language aims to make Lisp-grade macros approachable without Lisp notation, offering a dynamic core with static, abstraction-enforcing constructs and features like ellipsis-based repetitions for code that scales from scripts to large systems.

title: Algorithmic Monocultures in Hiring
blurb: A Stanford study tracked 3.4M people submitting 4M applications across 150 employers, all screened by a single third-party AI vendor, and found the tool produced racial bias that pooled metrics hide but per-position analysis exposes. Because 90% of US employers rely on the same few screening vendors, rejected candidates get shut out everywhere at once, a monoculture effect that cost Black and Asian applicants roughly 40,000 advancements.

title: Sources: Google AI researchers Jonas Adler and Alexander Pritzel, both viewed internally as key contributors to Gemini, are planning to leave for Anthropic (Bloomberg)
blurb: Two Google researchers, Jonas Adler and Alexander Pritzel, both regarded internally as key contributors to Gemini, are planning to leave for Anthropic, according to Bloomberg sources. The departures mark another high-profile talent loss for Google's flagship model effort as Anthropic continues to pull senior research staff from rival frontier labs.

title: Trump says he no longer views Anthropic as a national security threat after G7 meeting with CEO
blurb: Trump told Axios he no longer considers Anthropic a national security threat and may ease restrictions on its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, reversing a three-month standoff with the company. The shift follows a G7 meeting with CEO Dario Amodei and comes days after the Commerce Department ordered Anthropic to seek US approval before foreign nationals could access its most powerful models.

title: Apple's A12 and A13 Chips Facing New Unpatchable Exploit
blurb: Security firm Paradigm Shift published usbliter8, an unpatchable BootROM exploit affecting Apple's A12 and A13 chips, covering iPhone XS through iPhone 11. The flaw lives in the USB controller hardware and cannot be fixed with a software update, so affected devices stay vulnerable for life, extending the lineage of 2019's checkm8 to a newer generation of silicon.

title: claude-sonnet-5
blurb: The slug "claude-sonnet-5" surfaced on an Anthropic partner provider, suggesting the next Sonnet model is close to release. Anthropic has not confirmed anything, but provider-side identifiers typically appear days before a launch, pointing to a possible announcement in the coming week.

title: Ethereum Foundation Cuts 20% of Staff in Sweeping Reorganization
blurb: The Ethereum Foundation cut 54 employees, roughly 20% of staff, concluding a months-long reorganization tied to its updated treasury policy. Vitalik Buterin said the cuts support a 40% budget reduction this year, part of a plan to lower annual operating expenses from 15% of treasury toward a 5% baseline by 2030, as the Foundation reorganizes into five domains spanning protocol, access, user, community, and institutional layers.

title: OpenAI Expands Daybreak With GPT-5.5-Cyber to Help Defenders Patch Security Flaws
blurb: OpenAI released an improved GPT-5.5-Cyber model to trusted defenders under its Daybreak initiative, calling it its strongest model yet for finding and patching software vulnerabilities across large codebases. The company also updated its Codex Security plugin to triage scanner findings and generate patches at scale, and launched Patch the Planet with Trail of Bits to secure open-source projects including cURL, Python, and the Go project.

title: Towards a world where no one is surprised by a natural disaster
blurb: Google shared updates on its AI crisis-resilience work, including Flood Hub forecasts that now cover 2 billion people across more than 150 countries, with river floods predicted up to seven days out and new flash-flood warnings giving 24-hour notice in urban areas. The company open-sourced its flash-floods dataset and hydrology framework, and detailed WeatherNext 2, a model that generates hourly global weather forecasts in minutes.

title: Join Google Meet calls from Safari on iOS devices
blurb: iOS users without the Gmail or Meet app installed can now join Google Meet calls directly in Safari.

title: One Index for Subsumption and Roll-up across Time, Geography, and Ontology
blurb: OEH is a single index serving both subsumption tests and hierarchical roll-up across time, geospatial, and ontology hierarchies.

title: "Fix" MacBook Neo Cursor Lag: Record 1 Pixel of the Screen Every 10 Seconds
blurb: A developer works around MacBook Neo cursor lag by screen-recording one pixel every 10 seconds to keep the compositor on software cursor.

title: AI's Affordability Crisis
blurb: AI platforms are subsidizing token usage at staggering rates, with analysis showing a $200-a-month subscription can burn $8,000 in Anthropic tokens or $14,000 in OpenAI tokens. The author compiles mounting evidence that platforms spend $8 to $14 to generate $1 in revenue, echoing warnings from Sequoia's David Cahn and Ed Zitron that the economics remain deeply unsustainable.

title: Patch the Planet: a Daybreak initiative to support open source maintainers
blurb: OpenAI introduced Patch the Planet, a Daybreak initiative built with Trail of Bits that pairs AI-assisted security research with expert human review to find and fix vulnerabilities in critical open-source projects. Initial participants include cURL, Python, the Go project, pyca/cryptography, and aiohttp, with Trail of Bits committing its full research organization and partners HackerOne and Calif handling triage and coordinated disclosure.

title: Codex-maxxing for long-running work
blurb: An OpenAI whitepaper on using Codex as a persistent workspace that preserves context across long-running, multi-step projects.

title: Texas lassoes massive Microsoft datacenter - and 20 years of gas turbine emissions
blurb: Texas approved a massive Microsoft datacenter that will draw power from on-site gas turbines permitted to run for the next 20 years. The arrangement locks in two decades of fossil-fuel emissions to feed AI compute demand, underscoring how datacenter buildouts are outpacing grid capacity and pushing operators toward dedicated gas generation rather than clean-energy sources.

title: Ukraine puts its Russian war trophies online for allies to pick apart
blurb: Ukraine published technical breakdowns of captured Russian weaponry online, opening its trove of battlefield trophies for allied militaries and engineers to study. The catalog details components, electronics, and design choices found in seized hardware, turning frontline salvage into shared intelligence that NATO partners can use to understand Russian capabilities and harden their own systems.

title: Qualcomm lands Meta as first named customer for its Dragonfly data centre chips
blurb: Qualcomm signed Meta as the first named customer for its Dragonfly C1000 data centre processor, due in 2028, marking its most serious push yet into the AI infrastructure market. The company announced the deal at its investor day alongside a new AI300 inference accelerator and a confirmed $3.9B all-stock acquisition of AI software startup Modular.

title: Gemini 3.5 Flash can now see and control your screen, and Google wants enterprises to trust it
blurb: Google made computer use a built-in tool in Gemini 3.5 Flash, letting agents see, click, type, and scroll across browsers, mobile devices, and desktops without calling the separate Gemini 2.5 model it replaces. The capability ships through the Gemini API and the renamed Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, with targeted adversarial training against prompt injection as Google's central pitch for enterprise trust.

title: Alphabet added to Dow Jones Industrial Average, replacing Verizon
blurb: Alphabet will replace Verizon in the Dow Jones Industrial Average before Monday's open, S&P Global said, joining Nvidia, Amazon, Apple, and Microsoft among the index's mega-cap tech members. The move deepens the price-weighted Dow's exposure to AI, cloud, and advertising, and follows a heavy spending stretch in which Alphabet raised $141B in debt and equity since October.

title: SpaceX raises $25 billion in debt sale less than two weeks after IPO
blurb: SpaceX raised $25B in a five-tranche senior unsecured notes offering less than two weeks after its record IPO, drawing nearly $90B in orders at rates from 5.35% to 6.65%. Proceeds will repay a $20B bridge loan and fund Starship, Starlink, and AI efforts, including the $60B all-stock acquisition of coding startup Cursor, leaving SpaceX with over $100B in cash.

title: Mistral launches OCR 4, turning document extraction into a full enterprise AI play
blurb: Mistral released OCR 4, a document intelligence model that returns structured representations of entire documents with bounding boxes, block-type classification, and per-word confidence scores rather than flat text. The model supports 170 languages, deploys as a single container on a customer's own infrastructure for regulated industries, and starts at $4 per 1,000 pages, dropping to $2 via batch API.

title: MGX’s $50B Raise Sets Up One of the Biggest AI Funds Ever
blurb: Abu Dhabi's MGX raised close to $50B from regional and global investors, setting up one of the largest AI funds ever assembled. The state-backed firm will direct the capital toward AI infrastructure and technology, deepening Gulf sovereign wealth's push to become a primary financier of the global compute buildout.

title: Tata cyberattack allegedly exposes confidential Apple documents
blurb: Tata Electronics confirmed a cyberattack in which the World Leaks group claims to have stolen and leaked confidential documents from customers Apple and Tesla. The dump allegedly spans more than 200,000 files and 630GB, including iPhone component inspection standards and employee passport copies, with Tata now facing a ransom demand and Apple running a full analysis.

title: Nvidia unveils Vera Rubin platform targeting AI, HPC infrastructure
blurb: Nvidia launched the Vera Rubin platform, a rack-scale supercomputer that combines its Vera CPUs, Rubin GPUs, NVLink-C2C interconnects, and BlueField-4 DPUs with direct liquid cooling and up to 144 GPUs per rack. A fully configured system delivers more than seven exaflops of AI performance and five petaflops of FP64 compute, targeting scientific workloads like climate modeling, fluid dynamics, and quantum chemistry.

title: Meta launches AI glasses with three new styles from $299
blurb: Meta launched Meta Glasses, a line of AI eyewear built with EssilorLuxottica that starts at $299 and ships in three frame styles, including a Kylie Jenner collaboration, with 26 style variations and prescription support. The glasses run Meta AI on the new Muse Spark model for multimodal queries and 14 added live-translation languages, and offer hands-free capture, open-ear audio, and 8-plus hours of battery.

title: Micron invests in Anthropic and grants it a supply deal
blurb: Micron made a multi-year supply deal with Anthropic for HBM, DRAM, and SSDs while also investing in the AI lab's Series H round, alongside fellow memory makers Samsung and SK Hynix. The arrangement ties Anthropic's compute scaling to guaranteed memory allocations and includes joint work on storage performance and token economics, with Micron deploying Claude across its own engineering and manufacturing functions.

title: Cloudflare forms pact with browser giants to protect your privacy with tokens
blurb: Cloudflare partnered with Mozilla, Google, and Microsoft on Private Access Tokens (PACT), a standard that lets sites issue anonymous tokens proving a visitor is human without cookies or CAPTCHAs. The companies frame PACT as preparation for an agentic web where AI agents outnumber human users, giving sites a way to tell people from machines without tracking browsing history.

title: After betting the firm on Anthropic, Menlo Ventures raises victorious $3B fund
blurb: Menlo Ventures raised a $3B fund, the largest in its 50-year history, powered largely by its AI portfolio and an Anthropic stake now worth roughly $14B. The firm's 2024 bet-the-firm move, a $750M Series D investment assembled mostly through a $500M special purpose vehicle, has paid off as Anthropic's valuation climbed through subsequent rounds.

title: Drones startup Stark confirms €500m raise backed by Sequoia and Founders Fund
blurb: Berlin-based defence startup Stark confirmed a €500M raise to expand R&D and manufacturing across Europe, backed by Sequoia, Founders Fund, the NATO Innovation Fund, and others. The 2024-founded company, which reached a $1B-plus valuation in January, will put over 80% of the capital into R&D and production, scaling toward thousands of drone systems per month.

title: Anthropic Backer Menlo Ventures Raises $3B In New Funds To Back AI Startups Across Stages
blurb: Menlo Ventures raised $3B across two new funds, the largest raise in the firm's 50-year history, to back AI startups across enterprise, healthcare, and consumer at every stage. The capital splits into Menlo Ventures XVII for seed and Series A bets and Menlo Inflection IV for growth-stage companies, building on the firm's early stake in Anthropic, which has filed for a 2026 IPO targeting $1T or more.

title: Publicité : WALMART rachète VIBE pour 1,2 milliard d’euros afin d’accélérer sa riposte face à Amazon
blurb: Walmart is acquiring French adtech firm Vibe.co for about $1.4B, including $1.2B to shareholders and a roughly $180M earn-out to retain leadership, per the Wall Street Journal. The deal extends Walmart Connect into connected TV two years after the $2.3B Vizio purchase, as the retailer builds an advertising business to rival Amazon Ads and its tens of billions in annual revenue.

title: Xiaomi's HarnessX rewrites its own AI scaffolding mid-task — and smaller models gain the most
blurb: Xiaomi researchers introduced HarnessX, a framework that treats the AI harness, the prompts, tool integrations, memory, and control flows around a model, as a composable object and autonomously rewrites its own code mid-task using execution data. Across 15 model-benchmark combinations it averaged a 14.5% performance gain, with the open-weight Qwen3.5-9B improving 44% on embodied planning, showing that smaller models often benefit more than scaled-up backbones.

title: Show HN: TikZ Editor – WYSIWYG editor for figures in LaTeX
blurb: TikZ Editor is a browser-based WYSIWYG tool for building TikZ figures and exporting the LaTeX code. It lets users drag, position, and style diagram elements visually instead of writing TikZ markup by hand, then copy the generated source into a document.

title: What we call "age verification" is actually mass surveillance
blurb: Cory Doctorow argues that internet "age verification" is mass surveillance in disguise, forcing everyone to submit to fine-grained tracking of their online activity just to prove they are old enough. He notes the irony that the anti-Big Tech and culture-warrior coalitions pushing these mandates are unwitting allies of the surveillance-advertising industry, whose dream is a world where opting out of tracking is illegal.

title: p99 0 ms* autocomplete for 240 million domain names
blurb: Engineer prefetches autocomplete suggestions on keydown so results for 240M domains render before the user releases the key, hitting p99 ~0ms.

title: Qualcomm Says Data Center Chips to Produce ‘Billions’ in ’27
blurb: Qualcomm said its new data center AI chips will generate "billions" in revenue in fiscal 2027, which starts in October, marking a foothold in a market dominated by Nvidia. The company also added Meta as a customer for the chips, signaling demand from hyperscalers looking to diversify their AI hardware suppliers.

title: Show HN: Nub – A Bun-like all-in-one toolkit for Node.js
blurb: Nub is a Rust-built, Bun-like toolkit that augments stock Node.js with TypeScript, faster installs, and version management.

title: Anthropic debuts Claude Tag, a more capable AI teammate that lives within Slack
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, a Slack-based agent that breaks tasks into steps, completes them independently, and posts results back to the relevant channel. It builds on Claude Code and Claude Cowork by adding persistent memory and a shared organizational identity, so teammates can hand off half-finished work and administrators can scope each agent to specific channels, tools, and projects.

title: PR spam today looks like email spam in the early 2000s
blurb: Greptile argues that AI-generated pull request spam now resembles early-2000s email spam, pointing to OpenClaw, where weekly PRs jumped from two to 3,400 and merge rates fell from 48% to under 9.3% after coding agents flooded the repo. The author expects open source to adopt email's defenses: blocklists, confidence-based filters, and sender-reputation systems like Mitchell Hashimoto's Vouch, which gates contributions behind earned trust.

title: Why the Frontier Ecosystem must be Open — Matei Zaharia and Reynold Xin, Databricks
blurb: Databricks cofounders Matei Zaharia and Reynold Xin argue that the layer above coding agents should be open, and detail Omnigent, their open-source meta-harness for combining, controlling, and sharing agents across Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and internal tools. They also lay out Lakebase and LTAP, a bet that unifying the storage layer beats collapsing every query engine, and why databases matter more once agents start doing real work.

title: Oracle’s 21,000 layoffs help drive its debt-fueled AI investments
blurb: Oracle cut 21,000 jobs over the past year, a 12.9% reduction to 141,000 employees, citing AI adoption and a pivot to cloud infrastructure in its annual SEC filing. The company plans to raise $45B to $50B in 2026, about half through debt, to expand Oracle Cloud Infrastructure for customers like OpenAI, xAI, and Meta, pushing its total debt past $120B.

title: Anthropic’s Claude Tag aims to turn workplace AI from a personal assistant into a teammate
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, a Slack channel-based AI collaborator for its Enterprise and Team customers that retains context across conversations and works alongside multiple employees. Replacing the single-user Claude in Slack, Tag handles tasks autonomously with reviewable logs, schedules its own follow-up work over hours or days, and offers an ambient mode that surfaces relevant updates from connected channels and tools.

title: Agility Robotics plans to go public via SPAC in a $2.5B deal
blurb: Agility Robotics plans to go public through a merger with Churchill Capital Corp XI, valuing the humanoid robotics startup at roughly $2.5B and generating more than $620M in proceeds. The company, backed by Amazon, Nvidia, and SoftBank Vision Fund 2, will use the capital to scale production of its next-generation Digit v5, against more than $300M in secured multi-year orders and a pipeline of over 30 potential customers.

title: Meta announces new smart glasses starting at $299, as Zuckerberg keeps pushing wearables
blurb: Meta released new smart glasses starting at $299, roughly $80 below its entry-level second-generation Ray-Ban model, built with EssilorLuxottica but without Ray-Ban or Oakley branding. The screenless device pairs a camera and speakers with Meta's AI for translation and visual queries, as Google's Warby Parker partnership and Snap's $2,195 Specs intensify competition in a market Meta still dominates with over 80% share.

title: AI memory startup focused on cutting token costs raises $98 million
blurb: Engram, an 8-month-old AI memory startup, raised $98M from General Catalyst, Kleiner Perkins, Sequoia, and Anthropic's Andrej Karpathy to cut enterprise token costs. The company builds a "learned memory" layer that recalls organization-specific workflows and context, claiming it can match frontier labs while using up to 100 times fewer tokens, with early customers including Microsoft, Notion, and Harvey.

title: FCC plans ID mandate that could block anonymous use of prepaid burner phones
blurb: The FCC is seeking comment on a proposal requiring phone companies to collect and retain customers' names, physical addresses, and government ID numbers before granting service, a Know-Your-Customer expansion pitched as an anti-robocall measure. Privacy groups and domestic violence advocates warn the rules would effectively end anonymous prepaid burner phones, cutting off a safety tool that survivors fleeing abusers rely on to conceal their identity and location.

title: Meta Has Created a Prediction Markets App
blurb: Meta has built an experimental prediction markets app, internally called Arena, that would operate independently of Facebook and Instagram. The product would put Meta in direct competition with Polymarket and Kalshi, the largest players in a prediction market space that has drawn growing attention and regulatory scrutiny.

title: Rocket Lab launches satellite for U.S. Space Force Victus Haze responsive space exercise
blurb: Rocket Lab launched the Victus Haze "Puma" spacecraft for the U.S. Space Force aboard an Electron rocket from New Zealand on June 19, opening a military exercise to rapidly identify and characterize threats in orbit. Built under a $32M contract, the satellite will conduct rendezvous and proximity operations against a craft from startup True Anomaly, the fourth demo in the Space Force's push to make responsive launch an operational capability.

title: Chinese spaceplane releases object into orbit, according to commercial space surveillance
blurb: China's Shenlong reusable spaceplane released an unknown object into orbit during its fourth mission, according to commercial tracking firm LeoLabs, which cataloged it with high confidence from radar observations. The release follows a pattern from the secretive program's earlier flights, which featured rendezvous and proximity operations with deployed subsatellites, mirroring the behavior of the US Space Force's X-37B.

title: SpaceX to test upcoming Starfall reentry vehicle with demonstration mission on Tuesday
blurb: SpaceX will launch a demonstration of its Starfall reentry vehicle early Tuesday on a Falcon 9 from Cape Canaveral, deorbiting the disk-shaped capsule for a Pacific splashdown hours after liftoff. Starfall is a mass-produced 2,100 kg spacecraft built to autonomously return up to 1,000 kg of payload from orbit, targeting point-to-point cargo and in-orbit manufacturing once operational.

title: LastPass notifies users of yet another data breach
blurb: LastPass is notifying users of a breach at market-research partner Klue that exposed CRM contact data, though encrypted password vaults were unaffected.

title: How Meta Engineered Ultra-Narrow Batteries for AI Glasses
blurb: Meta engineered steel-can batteries as narrow as 7mm to power its Ray-Ban and Oakley smart glasses, replacing the wound "jelly roll" electrode with die-cut stacked layers to cut impedance and avoid brownouts when the camera and AI run at once. The rigid cells hold their shape to within 100 microns, reclaiming volume that pouch cells waste and lifting Gen 2 Ray-Ban capacity from 160 mAh to 210 mAh.

title: In memory of the man who put red and green squiggles under words
blurb: Tony Krueger, a longtime Microsoft engineer who shipped more versions of Word than perhaps anyone, made spell-check unobtrusive and introduced the red squiggles that mark misspelled words as you type, later joined by green squiggles for grammar.
